terms.definition: Each complete to-and-fro motion is one oscillation.
The time taken for one complete oscillation.
Physical quantities that have only magnitude.
Physical quantities that have both magnitude and direction.
The distance moved per unit time.
The rate of change of displacement.
Assumes that an object travels at the same speed throughout the entire distance.
Assumes that an object travels at the same velocity throughout the entire displacement.
The rate of change of velocity.
A constant rate of change of velocity.
Amount of matter in a body.
The gravitational force acting on an object that has mass.
A region in which a mass experiences a gravitational force.
Gravitational force per unit mass placed at that point.
States that every object will continue in its state of rest or uniform motion in a straight line unless a resultant force acts on it.
The reluctance of an object to change its state of rest or motion, due to its mass.
States that when a resultant force acts on an object of a constant mass, the object will accelerate in the direction of the resultant force.
States that if body A exerts a force on body B, then body B will exert an equal and opposite force of the same type on body A.
The contact force that opposes or tends to oppose motion between surfaces in contact.
When the air resistance acting against an object equals its weight, the object starts to travel at a constant speed known as terminal velocity.
The product of the force and the perpendicular distance from the pivot to the line of action of the force.
States that when a body is in equilibrium, the sum of clockwise moments about a pivot is equal to the sum of anticlockwise moments about the same pivot.
An imaginary point where the entire weight of the object seems to act.
A measure of its ability to maintain its original position.
The force acting per unit area.
Mass per unit volume.
If a pressure is applied to an enclosed liquid, the pressure is transmitted to all other parts of the liquid undiminished.
Energy cannot be created or destroyed. Energy can be transferred from one store to another. Total energy in an isolated system remains constant.

terms.term: Work done by a constant force
terms.definition: The product of the force and the distance moved by the object in the direction of the force.
Work done / Energy per unit time.
Is made up of tiny particles that are in continuous motion.
The random motion of particles in a fluid.
Describes a state in which two or more objects have the same temperature and that there is no net transfer of energy between them.
A process of energy transfer where energy is transferred through the passing on of vibrational motion from one particle to another.
A process of energy transfer by means of convection currents of a fluid (liquid or gas), due to a difference in density.
The process of energy transfer by electromagnetic waves. It does not require a medium.
Consists of the kinetic energy associated with the random motion of the particles and the total potential energy between the particles in the system.
The change of its internal energy per unit change in its temperature.
The change of its internal energy per unit mass for each unit change in its temperature.
The energy released or absorbed to change the state of a substance, at constant temperature.
The amount of energy transferred to change a substance between the solid and liquid states, at constant temperature.
The amount of energy transferred per unit mass of a substance to change between the solid and liquid states, at constant temperature.
The amount of energy transferred to change a substance between the liquid and gaseous states, at constant temperature.
The amount of energy transferred per unit mass of a substance to change it between the liquid and gaseous states, at constant temperature.
A disturbance that propagates through space, carrying energy with it but not matter.
The periodic and repetitive motion of any selected point in a wave.
Has a direction of vibration that is perpendicular to the direction of wave travel.
Has a direction of vibration that is parallel to the direction of wave travel.
A vector pointing from its rest position to the point.
Its maximum magnitude of displacement from its rest position.
Crests are the highest points of a transverse wave. Troughs are the lowest points of a transverse wave.
Describes two points on a wave that always have the same direction of motion (moving in step).
The shortest distance between two points which are in phase along the wave's direction.
The time taken by each point on the wave to complete one oscillation.
The number of oscillations each point completes per second.
The distance travelled by a wave per second.
An imaginary line joining all adjacent points that are in phase.
A longitudinal wave travelling in the air, created by a vibrating source.
Frequencies above 20 000 Hz.
The repetition of a sound due to the reflection of sound.
Those with higher frequencies and hence higher energies.
States that the incident ray, reflected ray and the normal at the point of incidence lie in the same plane.
States that the angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection.
The bending of light as light passes from one optical medium to another.
States that the incident ray, refracted ray and the normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane.
States that for two given media, the ratio of the sine of angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is a constant.
Defined as the ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in that medium.

terms.term: Principle of reversibility of light rays
terms.definition: States that regardless of how many times a light ray has been reflected or refracted, it will follow the same path when its direction is reversed.
Defined as the angle of incidence in an optically denser medium for which the angle of refraction in the less dense medium is 90 degrees.
The complete reflection of a light ray in an optically denser medium at the boundary with an optically less dense medium.
The distance between the optical centre and the principal focus point.
A region in which an electric charge experiences an electric force.
The rate of flow of charge with respect to time.
The direction of flow of positive charges is described as the conventional current.
The work done by the source in driving a unit charge around a complete circuit.
The work done per unit charge in driving charges through the component and that it is measured in volts.
The ratio of the potential difference across it to the current flowing through it.
A voltage divider, which makes use of voltage drop across resistors in series to divide voltage.
Convert changes in the physical condition, such as pressure, temperature or light into electrical signals so that these physical conditions can be measured.
Can take place when a ferromagnetic material is placed close to a strong magnet or within a current-carrying solenoid.
A region in which the force of magnetism acts.
Magnets that retain their magnetism in the presence of an electric current or a permanent magnetic field.
Do not require the presence of an electric current or a permanent magnetic field to retain their magnetism.
A current-carrying conductor will experience a force if the magnetic field applied is perpendicular (or has a component that is perpendicular) to the direction of flow of current.
The process through which an induced e.m.f. is produced in a conductor due to a changing magnetic field.
States that the magnitude of the induced e.m.f. in a circuit is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux in the circuit.
The magnetic field in a given area.
States that the direction of the induced e.m.f. and hence the induced current in a closed circuit, is always such that its magnetic effect opposes the motion or change producing it.
A device used in the transmission of electricity.
The number of protons in an atom.
The total number of neutrons and protons in the nucleus of an atom.
Atoms of the same element that have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
A random process by which an unstable atomic nucleus loses its energy by emission of electromagnetic radiation or particle(s).
Radiation with high energies that can knock off electrons from atoms to form ions.
Refers to nuclear radiation in an environment where no radioactive source has been deliberately introduced.
The time taken for half the nuclei of that nuclide in any sample to decay.
A process in which the nucleus of an atom splits (usually into two parts) and releases a huge amount of energy.
A process in which two light atomic nuclei combine to form one heavier atomic nucleus and releases a huge amount of energy.
